back: Confidentiality (secrecy), Integrity (no unauthorized change), Availability (accessible when needed).
Categories: technical, managerial, operational, physical. Types: preventive, detective, corrective, deterrent, compensating, directive.
Never trust by location; verify every request explicitly and grant least privilege.
Authentication (who you are), Authorization (what you may access), Accounting (record of what you did).
Symmetric: one shared key, fast, for bulk data. Asymmetric: public/private key pair, used for key exchange and signatures.
Integrity — a fixed-length fingerprint that detects change. It is one-way and does not provide confidentiality.
Proof a party performed an action (e.g., a digital signature) so they cannot credibly deny it.
Layer independent controls so a failure in one layer is covered by others.

front: Separation of duties vs least privilege
back: Separation of duties splits a task so no one person completes it alone; least privilege limits each person to the access they need.

front: Phishing vs spear phishing vs whaling
back: Phishing: broad. Spear phishing: targeted at specific people. Whaling: targets executives.
Malware that encrypts files and demands payment for the key. Best defense: tested offline backups.
Well-resourced (often nation-state) actor seeking long-term, stealthy access for strategic goals.
Vishing: social engineering by voice/phone. Smishing: social engineering by SMS text message.
A trusted person misusing legitimate access — hard to catch because the access is authorized.
An exploited flaw with no available patch yet. Mitigate with layered controls, monitoring, and fast response.
Attacker intercepts/alters traffic between two parties. Mitigate with TLS and certificate validation.
Spraying: one common password across many accounts (avoids lockout). Brute force: many passwords against one account.
Compromising a trusted vendor/update to reach its customers. Defend with vendor vetting and update integrity checks.
An isolated segment for internet-facing services so their compromise doesn't expose the internal LAN.
Data at rest (storage), in transit (network), and in use (memory) — each needs its own protections.
Redundancy duplicates components; resilience is the broader ability to keep operating and recover from failure.
Creates an encrypted tunnel (IPsec or TLS) to protect data in transit over untrusted networks.
Stateful tracks connection state and allows return traffic; stateless evaluates each packet against rules in isolation.
Labeling data by sensitivity (e.g., Public/Internal/Confidential) to apply the right handling and controls.
Tokenization swaps data for unrelated tokens stored in a vault; encryption is reversible with a key.
Three copies, on two media types, with one copy off-site.

front: Hot vs warm vs cold site
back: Hot: ready with current data (minutes). Warm: partially ready (hours/days). Cold: space and power only.
Something you know, something you have, something you are (plus somewhere you are / something you do).
Preparation → Identification → Containment → Eradication → Recovery → Lessons learned.
Centralizes and correlates logs from many sources to detect, alert on, and investigate incidents.
Tracking, testing, and deploying vendor updates to close known vulnerabilities.
Credentialed scans log in for deeper, more accurate results (patches/config); non-credentialed see only the external view.
CVE: the unique identifier for a specific vulnerability. CVSS: its 0-10 severity score.
Endpoint Detection and Response — monitors endpoints for suspicious behavior and enables investigation and isolation.
Privileged Access Management — vaults, monitors, and rotates privileged credentials to limit standing admin access.
CRL: a downloadable list of revoked certificates. OCSP: real-time status check for a single certificate.
Accept, avoid, transfer (e.g., insurance), and mitigate (reduce likelihood or impact).
Policy = intent/rules; standard = specific requirements; procedure = step-by-step how-to.
RTO = target time to restore service. RPO = maximum acceptable data loss measured in time.
SLE = single loss cost. ARO = times per year. ALE = SLE x ARO (expected yearly loss).
A central list of risks with owner, likelihood, impact, and chosen treatment.

front: SLA vs MOU vs NDA vs BPA
back: SLA: measurable service commitments. MOU: non-binding understanding. NDA: confidentiality. BPA: business partnership terms.
Controller decides why/how data is processed; processor acts on its instructions; data subject is the individual.
Identifies critical processes and the impact of downtime to set recovery priorities (feeds RTO/RPO).
Due diligence: up-front investigation. Due care: ongoing, reasonable effort to maintain protections.
